flashcards.front: What is the difference between a half adder and a full adder?
flashcards.back: A half adder adds two 1-bit inputs (A, B) producing Sum and Carry. A full adder adds three inputs (A, B, Cin) producing Sum and Cout. The full adder can handle carry propagation, making it suitable for multi-bit addition.

flashcards.front: In VHDL, what do 'entity' and 'architecture' represent?
flashcards.back: Entity declares the interface (input/output ports) of a circuit. Architecture defines the internal behavior or structure. Example: entity HalfAdder declares ports A, B (in) and S, C (out); architecture Behavioral describes S <= A xor B.

flashcards.front: Write the Verilog expressions for a full adder's Sum and Cout.
flashcards.back:
Sum: S = A ^ B ^ Cin (3-input XOR)
Cout: Cout = (A & B) | (A & Cin) | (B & Cin) (majority function — carry is 1 when at least 2 inputs are 1)

flashcards.front: How is an N-bit ripple-carry adder constructed from full adders?
flashcards.back: N full adders are connected in series. The carry output (Cout) of each full adder feeds into the carry input (Cin) of the next higher-bit full adder. The first full adder receives the external carry-in. This creates a 'ripple' of carries from LSB to MSB.

flashcards.front: What is the Verilog 'generate' construct used for?
flashcards.back: The generate-for loop creates multiple instances of modules or assignments parameterically. Used for scalable designs like N-bit adders: genvar i; generate for(i=0; i<N; i=i+1) begin: FA full_adder fa(...); end endgenerate
